request.question: Which woman, known as "The Queen of Porn" was among those featured at VH1's Big in '06 award show?

title: Patrick Murphy (softball)
VH1 Big in '05 Awards
VH1 Big in '06 Awards
Spike Video Game Awards
Sick of the Studio '07
Tigertrap Records
.30-06 Springfield
MotoGP '06
Botanica (band)
Jenna Jameson
sentences: Patrick Murphy (born November 28, 1965, in Waterloo, Iowa, grew up in Fayette) is an American softball coach and the current head coach of the Alabama Crimson Tide Softball team.

Murphy built one of the most successful college softball programs, taking Alabama to eleven Women's College World Series appearances (2000, '03, '05, '06, '08, '09,'11, '12, '14, '15 & '16) since 2000, which ties for first in the NCAA along with the UCLA, both having made 11 appearances in that span.
He led the Crimson Tide to its first national championship in 2012.
Murphy has also served as the hitting coach for the Canadian National Team in the 2004 Olympics and as an assistant coach for the United States national softball team in the summer of 2009.
VH1's Big in '05 was an award show that aired on VH1 on December 4, 2005 on VH1 in the United States.
It is the annual VH1's Big in... Awards.
Fall Out Boy was one of the performers.
VH1's Big in '06 was an award show that aired on VH1 on December 3, 2006 on VH1 in the United States.
It was the annual VH1 Big Awards.

The show was hosted by comedian D. L. Hughley, and featured many guests, including Paris Hilton, Hulk Hogan, Janelle Pierzina, Will Kirby, Danny Bonaduce, Justin Timberlake, Fergie, The Killers, the Fray, "Weird Al" Yankovic, Flavor Flav, Tiffany "New York" Pollard, Britney "Tiger" Morano, Abigail "Red Oyster" Kintanar, Jesselynn "Wire" Desmond, Jenna Jameson, Hayden Panettiere, Tommy Lee, Katharine McPhee, George Takei, Miley Cyrus, Masi Oka, Eva Longoria, David Hasselhoff, Mario Lopez, Joey Lawrence, will.i.am, Perez Hilton, lonelygirl15, Kiefer Sutherland, Xzibit and Dominic Monaghan, and many other celebrities associated with today's pop culture, most being reality tv stars.
The show was broadcast across the United States.

The Spike Video Game Awards (also known as the VGAs, and the VGX in its final year) were an annual award show hosted by Spike TV between 2003 and 2013 that recognized the best computer and video games of the year.
The VGAs featured live music performances and appearances by popular performers in music, movies, and television.
Additionally, preview trailers for upcoming games were highlighted.
The show was produced by "GameTrailers TV's" Geoff Keighley.
The event has been held at various locations in Los Angeles and Santa Monica, California as well as Las Vegas, Nevada.
The first event was held on December 2, 2003 (aired on December 4) while the last event was held on December 7, 2013.
Spike's only Video Game Hall of Fame award, given to "The Legend of Zelda", was awarded at the 2011 awards show.
On November 15, 2013, Spike TV announced a new format under the name "VGX", calling it "The next generation of the VGAs".
The last award show, carrying this name, aired on December 7.
Changes from the previous format included "in-depth extended demos of the next generation of games and interactive one-on-one interviews and panels in an intimate studio setting."
Sick of the Studio '07 was a 2007 concert tour by American heavy metal band Metallica, which took place in Europe.
The first four concerts were in festivals and the last eight in stadiums.
The band held a tourname-competition among the fans and they personally chose the winning suggestion.

The band did not play the "New Song" but the "Other New Song", which they had played on the previous tour (Escape from the Studio '06), was played at the Bilbao BBK Live Festival The setlists of the tour were full of old songs.
In Lisbon, Metallica performed "...And Justice For All" for the first time since October 1989.
Tigertrap Records is a London-based record label, which was set up in late 2005 by Drowned In Sound writers Tom Edwards and Adie Nunn.
The label released a series of singles through 2006, concentrating on unbroken talent.
During this time, former Warp Records employee Gill Barker joined to manage the label's business affairs.
Their first single, 586's "We Got Bored" became a sizeable indie club hit and NME awarded it 'Runner Up Single Of The Week' in May '06.
The second release was "Lowlife" by Scanners, which went on to be featured in a Jeff Bridges film, "Mama's Boy".
The band released their debut album on Dim Mak (home to Bloc Party, The Rakes, etc.).

The .30-06 Springfield cartridge (pronounced "thirty-aught-six" or "thirty-oh-six"), 7.62×63mm in metric notation and called ".30 Gov't '06" by Winchester, was introduced to the United States Army in 1906 and later standardized; it remained in use until the early 1980s.
The ".30" refers to the caliber of the bullet.
The "06" refers to the year the cartridge was adopted—1906.
It replaced the .30-03, 6mm Lee Navy, and .30-40 Krag cartridges.

The .30-06 remained the U.S. Army's primary rifle and machine gun cartridge for nearly 50 years before being replaced by the 7.62×51mm NATO (commercial .308 Winchester) and 5.56×45mm NATO, both of which remain in current U.S. and NATO service.
It remains a very popular sporting round, with ammunition produced by all major manufacturers.
MotoGP '06 is a Grand Prix motorcycle racing video game exclusively for the Xbox 360.
The developer and publisher say that it offers better graphics and more races than have been featured in previous games.
To make it more realistic enhanced lighting is used to show sun reflections come off the motorbikes and tracks.
New features include a new season mode, new riders, bikes and tracks.
The "extreme mode" featured in "" is enhanced for the Xbox 360.
In the predecessor 16 players could race online but now in this installment the player can race up to 20 players online.
Botanica is a New York based band, founded in Los Angeles in 1999, led by erstwhile Firewater co-conspirator, keyboardist/singer Paul Wallfisch.

Named for those mysterious stores stocking spells, potions and artifacts of ritual, Botanica features strings, musical saw, toy piano, omnichord, optigan and other odd keyboards and is known for a dynamic and eclectic mix of gypsy and punk-cabaret infused chamber rock.

Members and collaborators have included Brian Viglione (of The Dresden Dolls), Jason Binnick (The Flesh, Hallelujah), Miriam Eicher (Hallelujah, Cardiac Move), Abby Travis, (Beck, Elastica, KMFDM), Ivan Knight, (Stan Ridgeway), Daniel Ash, (Bauhaus, Love & Rockets), Frankie Infante, (Blondie), Kid Congo Powers, (Nick Cave, Cramps), Oren Kaplan, (Gogol Bordello), Matt Flynn, (Maroon 5), and Anne de Wolff, (Calexico, Neko Case).
Since 2002, the core of the band has been Wallfisch and guitarist/songwriter John Andrews, with bassist and vocalist Dana Schechter, (Bee and Flower), recently taking over for Christian Bongers.
Botanica has worked with various producers including Martin Bisi, Moses Schneider, Pete Min and Tamir Muskat.
"Malediction", the band's first album was released on Checkered Past Records in the U.S. and Cargo in Europe.
Botanica toured nationally from 1999-2002, including support dates with 16 Horsepower, Super Furry Animals and Rocket From the Crypt.
The band's subsequent albums have all been released exclusively in Europe, the last 3 through Germany's Rent A Dog label.
The third album, "Botanica vs. the Truth Fish", features a cover painting courtesy of renowned British artist Dexter Dalwood.
Among TV and radio appearances in several countries, the band has taped a full concert for the legendary Rockpalast show on Germany's WDR.
"Berlin Hi-Fi", Botanica's 4th album came out in May '06 and was featured as a Visions Magazine "Beauty of the Month."
Botanica has toured Europe twice a year since 2002, their last appearances in October 2007 presented by the German magazines Visions, Intro and Westzeit.

Botanica contributed the track ""Broken Bicycles"" to the Tom Waits tribute album "New Coat of Paint" released on Manifesto Records in 2002 and also featuring Screaming Jay Hawkins, Carla Bozulich, Lydia Lunch and Sally Norvell accompanied by Paul Wallfisch at the piano.
Botanica's music has been used in several films including "Men Cry Bullets" (Phaedra), and "The Takedown"(Dimension/Miramax).
Botanica, (with ex-Jesus Lizard David Sims), is featured in Greg Pritikin's film "Dummy" (Artisan Entertainment), starring Adrien Brody and Milla Jovovich.
Wallfisch also scored the film.

Jenna Jameson (born Jenna Marie Massoli; April 9, 1974) is an American entrepreneur, webcam model and former pornographic film actress, who has been called the world's most famous adult entertainment performer and "The Queen of Porn".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Agent role: Produce one compact second-hop retrieval query targeting the missing bridge or supporting evidence.

Pipeline position: after summary1 and before second-hop retrieval. Inputs are the original question and the first-hop intermediate state. Functional responsibility: produce one focused retrieval query that is likely to recover the missing evidence needed by the downstream agents. Query quality is determined by evidence recovery, not by whether the query itself resembles or contains the final answer.

Native output contract: The module must consume `question` and `summary_1` and produce exactly one compact second-hop query field named `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include every distinct entity token visible in the inputs in the single terse query and place at most one neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the targeted relation or attribute (for example: "EntityA EntityB director").
- If a visible disambiguator or attribute token appears adjacent to an entity in the inputs (for example a multiword service name, an explicit page title, a year, or an attribute marker like a birth/producer/service/series cue), include that disambiguator token together with the associated entity in the query rather than dropping or truncating it.
- If `summary_1` contains an explicit page-title token sequence (for example an instruction-like ExactPageTitle or Fetch page title), prefer that exact page-title token sequence as the query value.
- When the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise plain-text summary string (no extra fields, labels, markup, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification.
- Primary goal: explicitly integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the supported linkage it needs.

Explicit bridging requirement:
- When the passages supply a clear linking fact between the context entity and information in the passages, produce a concise 1–2 sentence integrated summary that: (1) names the context entity using the exact form present in `context`, (2) names the supporting entity or attribute using the exact form present in `passages`, and (3) states the explicit relation (membership, creator attribution, parentage, partnership, award, representation, founding, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Phrase this as one short bridging sentence when possible.
- For relations that naturally involve two distinct facts (for example, parentage plus representation, or a person linked to a show and also to an occupation), it is acceptable to use two short sentences within the same single-string summary: e.g., one sentence for parentage and one for representation. Still observe the 1–2 sentence limit.

Inclusion of passage-supplied disambiguators (only when directly relevant):
- If a passage supplies canonical multi-token names or other disambiguating attributes needed to resolve the question, include them inline using the wording from the passages: append birthdates in parentheses immediately after the associated full canonical name (e.g., Name (YYYY–) ), state group type and formation year using the passage's phrasing (e.g., "a [group type] formed in [year]"), and include a canonical member list exactly as shown (comma-separated) only when the passage presents a definitive membership list. Do not add ancillary attributes that are not necessary to the identification or explicit linking required by the question.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (e.g., "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages, comma-separated if multiple. Do not add explanatory clauses, relations, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (for example: "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Preserve uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 short sentences). Do not expand into biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Use only facts and phrasing present in `context` and `passages`; do not introduce or infer unsupported information.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.
- Do not assert that a passage's member list is complete unless the passage explicitly states it is definitive.

Format enforcement:
- Return a single plain-text string only. Do not emit JSON, dictionaries, labels, bullets, or any structured artifact—only the concise natural-language summary string.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else. Before emitting that string, you may apply only the narrowly permitted normalizations listed below. After applying permitted normalizations, the emitted string must equal the minimal label text from the prioritized summary verbatim (see normalization constraints). Specific allowed single-token answers: for a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no. For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces), subject only to the permitted normalizations. Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if a permitted normalization applies (see below).

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) and the permitted normalizations as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Permitted normalizations and extraction rules (apply only when the token(s) to emit appear verbatim in the prioritized summary and only to produce a strictly smaller, but still verbatim-supported, minimal label):
- Parenthetical/core-name stripping: if the candidate label in the prioritized summary includes common corporate suffixes or parenthetical abbreviations (for example variants like "Inc.", "LLC", or parenthetical acronyms), strip those suffixes/parentheticals and emit the core name only (do not invent or substitute other words).
- Hyphenated composite phrase extraction: if the prioritized summary presents a composite genre or label in the form "<modifier> X-Y" (for example an extra leading modifier followed by a hyphenated core like "classic action-adventure"), emit only the hyphenated core token exactly as shown ("X-Y").
- Trailing generic-descriptor removal: if the candidate minimal answer appears as a proper-name followed by a single trailing generic descriptor token of the form "<ProperName> crisis" (or an equivalent single-word generic classifier), strip the trailing generic token and emit only the proper-name sequence.
- Denomination-occupation reduction: if the question's scope requests a denomination/kind and the prioritized summary contains the form "<denomination> priest" (or equivalent denomination + occupational noun), emit only the denomination token(s) (i.e., the minimal denomination label).

Normalization constraints:
- Do not apply normalizations unless the text to emit appears verbatim in the prioritized summary (summary_2, or summary_1 only if summary_2 is silent for the answer).
- Normalizations may only remove or trim tokens; they must not add, invent, or substitute new content.
- Do not globally strip generic classifier nouns from multi-token geographic names or other proper nouns unless covered by the permitted rules above.

Tie-break and variant-selection rules:
- If a role-descriptor and a proper name co-occur in the prioritized summary in the form "<role> <Name>", prefer emitting the named individual exactly as shown (emit the Name only) unless the prioritized summary presents the role as the requested concise label explicitly without a name.
- For title or label-variant conflicts, prefer an alternate or parenthetical variant only when the prioritized summary explicitly marks that variant as an alias, alternate name, or the original-language title (or otherwise explicitly labels it as a variant) AND that variant matches the question's scope. If no such explicit labeling appears, emit the primary label supported by summary_2.

Strict emission rule (verbatim-emission):
- The final `answer` must equal the minimal label text from the prioritized summary verbatim after applying only the permitted normalizations above. Do not append or prepend occupational nouns, role descriptors, location qualifiers, or explanatory tokens that are not part of that verbatim label.

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.question: Which woman, known as "The Queen of Porn" was among those featured at VH1's Big in '06 award show?

Tigertrap Records

Jenna Jameson (born Jenna Marie Massoli; April 9, 1974) is an American entrepreneur, webcam model and former pornographic film actress, who has been called the world's most famous adult entertainment performer and "The Queen of Porn".

response.hop1_query: Which woman, known as "The Queen of Porn" was among those featured at VH1's Big in '06 award show?
hop1_docs: VH1 Big in '06 Awards | VH1's Big in '06 was an award show that aired on VH1 on December 3, 2006 on VH1 in the United States.  It was the annual VH1 Big Awards.  The show was hosted by comedian D. L. Hughley, and featured many guests, including Paris Hilton, Hulk Hogan, Janelle Pierzina, Will Kirby, Danny Bonaduce, Justin Timberlake, Fergie, The Killers, the Fray, "Weird Al" Yankovic, Flavor Flav, Tiffany "New York" Pollard, Britney "Tiger" Morano, Abigail "Red Oyster" Kintanar, Jesselynn "Wire" Desmond, Jenna Jameson, Hayden Panettiere, Tommy Lee, Katharine McPhee, George Takei, Miley Cyrus, Masi Oka, Eva Longoria, David Hasselhoff, Mario Lopez, Joey Lawrence, will.i.am, Perez Hilton, lonelygirl15, Kiefer Sutherland, Xzibit and Dominic Monaghan, and many other celebrities associated with today's pop culture, most being reality tv stars.  The show was broadcast across the United States.

VH1 Big in '05 Awards | VH1's Big in '05 was an award show that aired on VH1 on December 4, 2005 on VH1 in the United States.  It is the annual VH1's Big in... Awards.  Fall Out Boy was one of the performers.

Famous Food | Famous Food is a VH1 reality series that premiered 10 July 2011.  It features seven celebrities as they work to open and take ownership in a restaurant in Hollywood owned by Mike Malin (who competed in "Big Brother 2" and "Big Brother 7: All-Stars", which he won) and Lonnie Moore of The Dolce Group.  Due to low ratings, VH1 announced on July 15 that the show will move to Wednesdays 8/7c beginning July 20.  DJ Paul was the winner of the partnership but after a twist the judges awarded a second partnership to Danielle Staub.  The restaurant created on the show, named "Lemon Basket", was closed after just 5 months.

RuPaul's Drag Race (season 8) | The eighth season of RuPaul's Drag Race began airing on March 7, 2016.  Returning judges include RuPaul and Michelle Visage, along with Ross Mathews and Carson Kressley, who shared judging responsibilities on an alternating basis.  Twelve drag queens competed for the title of "America's Next Drag Superstar".  The prizes for the winner are a one-year supply of Anastasia Beverly Hills cosmetics and a cash prize of $100,000.  The full list of contestants was revealed during the NewNowNext Awards on February 1, 2016.  The song "U Wear It Well" from RuPaul's album "Butch Queen" was featured in promotion for the show.  On March 31, 2016, it was announced LogoTV renewed the show for a ninth season.  It was the last season to solely be played on Logo, as the show moved to VH1 for its ninth season, and Season 2 was broadcast simultaneously on VH1.

One-Hit Wonders (VH1 TV series) | One-Hit Wonders is a television series airing on VH1 featuring unsuccessful pop stars and those who were unable to get a big break into popular music.  The series began airing on July 29, 2002.

Angela Raiola | Angela Joyce Raiola (June 30, 1960 – February 18, 2016), better known by her nickname Big Ang (pronounced "Anj") was an American reality television personality who co-starred in the VH1 series "Mob Wives".  Raiola was the niece of Salvatore "Sally Dogs" Lombardi (1941–2009), a caporegime ("capo") and drug dealer in the Genovese crime family.  Known as a “mob moll”, Raiola dated gangsters and lived an opulent lifestyle.  She joined the "Mob Wives" cast during the show's second season, in 2012.  The same year, after she became the show's breakout star, VH1 gave Raiola her own reality show, "Big Ang," which evolved into "Miami Monkey."  She has been described as a gay icon.

VH1's Legends | Legends (aka VH1's Legends) is a music biography television series on VH1.  Originally sponsored by AT&T Corporation, this series documents those artists (living or dead) who have made a significant contribution to music history to be profiled on the show (as opposed to VH1's companion series, "Behind The Music", which profiles mostly moderately significant artists).

hop2_docs: Jenna Jameson | Jenna Jameson (born Jenna Marie Massoli; April 9, 1974) is an American entrepreneur, webcam model and former pornographic film actress, who has been called the world's most famous adult entertainment performer and "The Queen of Porn".

How to Make Money Like a Porn Star | How to Make Money Like a Porn Star is the first graphic novel published by ReganBooks/HarperCollins, written by New York Times bestselling author Neil Strauss and illustrated by artist Bernard Chang.  Strauss and Chang have collaborated on two previous books, "How to Make Love Like a Porn Star" (the autobiography of porn queen Jenna Jameson), and "".

Shadow Hunter (comics) | Shadow Hunter is a three issue comic book limited series by Virgin Comics and porn star, entertainer and author Jenna Jameson.  It is part of Virgin Comics' Voices line, which allows celebrities to create their own comic books.  The story was conceptualized by Jenna Jameson and Witchblade writer Christina Z, who wrote the script.  Mukesh Singh illustrated the book.

Pornucopia | Pornucopia is an American documentary series by HBO spun off from "Real Sex" that focuses on the Californian porn industry.  It featured interviews with Jenna Jameson, Jenna Haze, Katie Morgan, Dave Cummings, Evan Stone, Jeff Stryker, Annabelle Travello and much more.

How to Make Love Like a Porn Star | How to Make Love Like a Porn Star: A Cautionary Tale is the autobiography of adult film star Jenna Jameson, published August 17, 2004.  It was mainly written by co-writer Neil Strauss, later famous for writing "", and published by ReganBooks, a division of HarperCollins.  It was an instant best-seller, spending six weeks on the New York Times Best Seller list.  The autobiography also won the 2004 "Mainstream's Adult Media Favorite" XRCO award in a tie with Seymore Butts's "Family Business" TV series.
Jenna Jameson filmography | The following provides a list of the film, television, music video, and video game performances of American pornographic actress Jenna Jameson.

ClubJenna | ClubJenna, Inc. was a multi-media adult entertainment company based in Scottsdale, Arizona.  It was founded in 2000 by adult film actress Jenna Jameson, and Jay Grdina, who performed as an adult film actor under the name Justin Sterling and today is president of ClubJenna, Inc.  Initially a single website, the business expanded into managing similar websites of other stars and began producing pornographic films in 2000.  The first such film, "Briana Loves Jenna" (with Briana Banks), was named at the 2003 AVN Awards as the best-selling and best-renting pornographic title for 2002.  Its assets include a film production business, a network of pay web sites, as well as a subscription-based cable television channel.  By 2005 Club Jenna had revenues of US$30 million with profits estimated at half that.  In 2006 it was described by Reuters as one of the handful of studios that dominate the U.S. porn industry.
